text: The surgeon places standard left laparoscopic donor ports with an anti-clockwise rotation, targeting the left adrenal lesion. The white line of Toldt is incised at the splenic flexure, and the plane between the mesocolon and the suprarenal mass is identified. The colon is deflected away, and the ligamentous attachments of the spleen are cut to medialize the spleen. An avascular plane is developed between the colon and the suprarenal mass.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut bands as they appear, extending the dissection cranially towards the adrenal mass. The medial end of the mass is carefully dissected away from the aortic margin. The dissection continues cranially, mindful of the pancreatic tail, until the psoas muscle is visible. The superior and supralateral margins of the adrenal mass are freed from the surrounding parietal tissue.

The surgeon deflects the colon medially and caudally to expose the renal and adrenal veins. Fibroareolar tissue layers over the renal vein are peeled away, revealing the adrenal vein. A window is created at the superior margin of the adrenal vein to identify the plane between the adrenal gland and the upper pole of the kidney. A surgical cell is placed over the adrenal vein to manage diffuse bleeding during plane creation. The inferior margin of the adrenal vein is delineated in preparation for clipping.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to sever the delineated adrenal vein between clips, exposing the psoas muscle from the medial margin. The supramedial parietal attachments are harmonized over a clip, and the superior margin of the adrenal mass is dissected free. Residual strands are harmonized and clipped as needed. The inferior attachments of the adrenal mass to the upper pole of the kidney are removed using the harmonic scalpel, with occasional application of clips.
